title: Data
text: The MC2 dataset represents Oceanus as a knowledge graph: entities are nodes, and relationships between them are edges. In this page, we break down the data, how we understood it, and the analytical decisions we made before working on the visualisations.

title: Data
text: The MC2 dataset represents Oceanus as a knowledge graph: entities are nodes, and relationships between them are edges. In this page, we break down the data, how we understood it, and the analytical decisions we made before working on the visualisations.

title: Data
text:
The files








File
Rows
Role in analysis




mc2_journalist_links.csv
2,436
The journalist links file has the complete source of truth to identify any bias based on the organisations’ claims. Every link is a relationship between nodes in the journalist_nodes file, filling us with the full picture.


mc2_jounalist_nodes.csv
740
The journalist nodes file contains every entity in the complete dataset: meetings, people, topics, and travel destinations. All FILAH and TROUT nodes are fully in the file.


mc2_FILAH_links.csv
765
FILAH’s submitted evidence: a cherry-picked subset of the journalist links file. Every record here exists in the journalist file; what differs is what FILAH chose to leave out to support their pro-tourism bias narrative.


mc2_FILAH_nodes.csv
396
FILAH’s node list, fully part of the journalist nodes file. Node-level comparisons are handled through the links files and the journalist nodes as the single authoritative reference.


mc2_TROUT_links.csv
378
TROUT’s submitted evidence: a different cherry-picked subset of the journalist links file. A smaller slice than FILAH’s dataset, curated to suppress travel evidence and overrepresent fishing activity in support of their own counter-narrative.


mc2_TROUT_nodes.csv
164
TROUT’s node list, fully part of the journalist nodes file. Notably smaller dataset, reflecting TROUT’s narrower evidence pool.


mc2_geo_nodes.csv
2,664
Physical locations in Oceanus with zone classification and coordinates. Used to enrich travel links and determine whether COOTEFOO members’ travel destinations skewed toward fishing or tourism zones.


mc2_geo_edges.csv
3,420
The road network connecting locations across Oceanus.

title: Data
text:
The graph structure
Each links file contains edges between nodes. Every row is one connection. The critical column is role, which defines what kind of connection it is.









Role
Count (journalist)
Source-Target
Analytical use




participant
218
Discussion → Member or Organisation
Primary analysis. Only role that carries sentiment scores.


about
200
Discussion → Topic
Topic coverage analysis


part_of
175
Discussion → Meeting
Meeting structure


plan
73
Topic → Plan document
Output tracking


travel
22
Topic → Geographic location
Travel pattern analysis


refers_to
43
Document → Document
Cross-reference network









Only 218 of 2,436 rows are participant type. The remaining 2,218 rows carry no sentiment values: 1,705 have no role assigned at all, and the other 513 belong to structural roles like about, part_of, and plan. Every sentiment analysis in this project is filtered exclusively to participant links. Forgetting this filter produces meaningless averages across thousands of NULL sentiment values.



Nodes contain: type, id, name, role, label,short_topic, long_topic, lat, lon, zone, zone_detail, date, start, end.
Node types present: entity.person, entity.organization, meeting, discussion, plan, trip, place, topic.

title: Data
text:
The COOTEFOO board members
Six people sit on the COOTEFOO board. They appear as target values in participant links. Their presence or absence across the three datasets is itself evidence of bias.

Member

FILAH recorded 3 members; TROUT recorded 3 members, but they share Seal in common. The two groups each omitted members that inconvenienced their narrative: FILAH dropped Ed Helpsford and Teddy Goldstein; TROUT dropped Simone Kat and Carol Limpet. Tante Titan, whose full record shows balanced, positive engagement across both industries, was excluded by both groups.

title: Data
text:
The critical finding
Before building any visualisation, we analysed the data using the three links files using (source + target + role).






Result: 100% of FILAH’s 765 rows exist inside the journalist file. 100% of TROUT’s 378 rows also exist inside the journalist file.
The journalist file is a complete superset. FILAH and TROUT did not collect independent data, they each drew from the same pool of 2,436 records and selectively chose what to submit.






Segment
Count




In FILAH only (not TROUT)
586


In TROUT only (not FILAH)
199


In both FILAH and TROUT
179


In Journalist only (neither group)
1,472


Total unique journalist links
2,436



1,472 records: 60% of the journalist’s dataset were not presented by either activist group.

title: Data
section: Understanding what “bias” means in this data
text:
Understanding what “bias” means in this data
The data does not contain a direct “bias” column. Bias must be inferred from patterns. We identified three layers:

Layer 1 — Sentiment bias
participant links carry a sentiment score from −1 (very negative) to +1 (very positive). If one group’s data shows systematically different sentiment toward fishing vs tourism topics, that is evidence of bias in the data they chose to submit.



Dataset
Fishing sentiment
Tourism sentiment




FILAH
0.334
0.679


TROUT
0.400
0.191


Full Journalist
0.449
0.531



FILAH’s data makes the board look strongly pro-tourism. TROUT’s data makes the board look strongly pro-fishing. The full dataset shows near-equal engagement with both. The gap between each group’s view and reality is the quantified sampling bias.


Layer 2 — Topic omission bias
Two topics are completely absent from one group’s data:



FILAH omitted:
concert: a Tourism topic
Present in TROUT and Journalist
Missing while accusing the board of pro-tourism bias


TROUT omitted:
seafood_festival: a Fishing topic
Present in FILAH and Journalist
Missing while accusing the board of pro-fishing bias



These are not gaps from random sampling. Each group removed the topic that goes against their accusation.


Layer 3 — Member exclusion bias
Beyond topics, the groups excluded entire people:



FILAH excluded:

Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.85, Tourism=−0.50)

Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0)

Tante Titan (balanced)



TROUT excluded:
1. Simone Kat (Tourism=0.91)
2. Carol Limpet
3. Tante Titan (balanced)



The pattern is consistent: each group excluded members whose records would have weakened their narrative and included those whose records strengthened it.




FILAH excluded


TROUT excluded






Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.786, Tourism=−0.500) Strong pro-fishing, anti-tourism record would have undermined FILAH’s narrative


Simone Kat (Fishing=0.379, Tourism=0.908) Strongly pro-tourism record would have undermined TROUT’s narrative




Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0, Tourism=0.714) Uniformly positive across both industries, not a useful villain


Carol Limpet (Fishing=0.750, Tourism=0.682) Balanced across both industries, not useful for TROUT’s case either




Tante Titan (Fishing=0.750, Tourism=0.471) Both excluded: Positive and balanced across both industries; directly contradicts both groups’ bias claims

title: Data
section: How we structured the data in Tableau
text:
How we structured the data in Tableau
The journalist links file became the single base table. FILAH and TROUT links were left-joined onto it using a two-field composite key.
Base: mc2_journalist_links.csv (2,436 rows)
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_FILAH_links.csv  ON source + target 
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_TROUT_links.csv  ON source + target 
After the join, rows where FILAH’s source column is NULL were excluded by FILAH. Rows where TROUT’s source column is NULL were excluded by TROUT. Two boolean calculated fields (In FILAH, In TROUT) capture this. A parameter (Dataset View) switches the filter between FILAH, TROUT, and Full Journalist perspectives without requiring separate data sources.
The journalist nodes file was added twice as related tables — once for target node attributes, once for source node attributes. The geo nodes file was added as a third relationship, keyed on target = id, resolving only for travel links where the target is a geographic node ID.






Why not union? Unioning all three links files would duplicate the 765 FILAH rows and 378 TROUT rows that already exist in the journalist file, inflating all counts by up to 3×. The join approach preserves exactly 2,436 rows.

title: Data
text:
Classification of Topics

fish_vacuum

low_volume_crane




seafood_festival

Tourism

marine_life_deck




waterfront_market




heritage_walking_tour




concert




Other


statue_john_smoth




renaming_park_himark




name_harbor_area




name_inspection_office










Topic classification was not provided in the data: we assigned each topic to an industry category based on the short-form topic description in the nodes file. The topics were grouped based on our assumption of fishing and tourism activites.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
The single most consequential technical discovery was that FILAH and TROUT’s datasets are subsets of the journalist file, not independent datasets. This is not stated anywhere in the challenge documentation. It required a trial and error to check if the data is accurate.



The practical consequence was significant: a naive union of all three files would have inflated every count by up to 3× and made all sentiment averages meaningless. Verifying dataset relationships before building anything is not a step to skip.



Tableau’s default data model keeps tables separate to avoid double-counting. That same separation means it cannot check, for a single row, whether that row exists in two different tables at once, which is exactly what our inclusion indicators (In FILAH, In TROUT) needed to do. We switched to a direct join instead. This let us ask “is this journalist record also in FILAH? Also in TROUT?” all in one place. The downside of a direct join is that it can inflate row counts if the data isn’t clean, so we verified the join keys were unique before committing to this approach. It worked here, but it’s a decision that depends on knowing your data first.



Both columns exist in the links CSVs and appear in Tableau’s Data pane. Both are completely empty. Using either one silently produces all-NULL output with no error message. This is a data quality issue that would not be caught without independently verifying field contents before building charts.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
The single most consequential technical discovery was that FILAH and TROUT’s datasets are subsets of the journalist file, not independent datasets. This is not stated anywhere in the challenge documentation. It required a trial and error to check if the data is accurate.



The practical consequence was significant: a naive union of all three files would have inflated every count by up to 3× and made all sentiment averages meaningless. Verifying dataset relationships before building anything is not a step to skip.



Tableau’s default data model keeps tables separate to avoid double-counting. That same separation means it cannot check, for a single row, whether that row exists in two different tables at once, which is exactly what our inclusion indicators (In FILAH, In TROUT) needed to do. We switched to a direct join instead. This let us ask “is this journalist record also in FILAH? Also in TROUT?” all in one place. The downside of a direct join is that it can inflate row counts if the data isn’t clean, so we verified the join keys were unique before committing to this approach. It worked here, but it’s a decision that depends on knowing your data first.



Both columns exist in the links CSVs and appear in Tableau’s Data pane. Both are completely empty. Using either one silently produces all-NULL output with no error message. This is a data quality issue that would not be caught without independently verifying field contents before building charts.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
2. Analytical Learnings

Absence of evidence is evidence






The most powerful findings in this project came from what was not there: the missing concert topic in FILAH, the missing seafood_festival in TROUT, the missing Tante Titan in both datasets.



Standard exploratory analysis focuses on what is present. For a bias investigation, systematically looking for absences is equally important. Heatmaps are particularly useful here because blank cells are visually salient in a way that missing rows in a table are not.


Sentiment scores don’t change, but dataset composition does
We initially expected to find that a member’s sentiment scores would look different in the partial datasets versus the full dataset. They do not — because each member’s records are exactly the same in the partial dataset as they are in the journalist dataset (since partial datasets are subsets).
What changes is which members are present. This is a subtle but important distinction: the bias mechanism is not in the scores, it is in the selection of whose scores to show. Slopegraph-style before/after charts would have been misleading here because the “before” and “after” values for included members are identical.


Individual variation is not board-level bias
Teddy Goldstein’s Tourism sentiment of −0.50 is a real result. But having one strong opinion on a board of six does not mean the whole board is biased, it just means one member has a strong personal view.
Both activist groups make the mistake of treating individual differences as if they reflect a pattern across the entire board.






The overall board averages (Fishing = 0.490, Tourism = 0.549) matter because they provide context.
Without these averages, Teddy Goldstein might look like evidence of a bigger problem. With them, it’s clear he is simply an outlier, not proof of board-wide bias.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
3. Project & Team Learnings

Validate data structure before any design work






We spent time building dashboard concepts before fully characterising the dataset. The discovery that FILAH and TROUT were subsets of the journalist file, not found until weird graphical representations came out, required redesigning the entire data source architecture.



Running a thorough data audit (row counts, overlap analysis, field completeness check, type validation) before opening Tableau would have saved significant time.


The four analytical questions map naturally to dashboard design
Breaking the project into four explicit questions (Q1 through Q4) gave us a clear brief for each dashboard. Charts that could not be traced to one of the four questions were either removed or reconsidered. This discipline helped us avoid building interesting-looking charts that did not contribute to the analytical narrative.


Choosing the right chart type matters more than visual polish
Several early dashboard iterations used chart types that were technically functional but analytically weak for the question being asked:

A diverging percentage chart for the travel data looked sophisticated but was misleading at small counts.
A stacked bar that summed three industry averages looked reasonable but was arithmetically meaningless.

The most impactful improvement to each dashboard came from asking “what is the specific claim this chart needs to support?” rather than “what would look good here?”

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
4. What We Would Improve

More explicit null handling in heatmaps
Blank cells in the heatmap currently represent either true zero counts or topics a member never engaged with. These are analytically different:



Zero count
Member engaged but count = 0
Should render as a distinct fill colour


NULL / absent
Combination does not exist in the data
Should render as white / empty



Distinguishing these more explicitly would make the heatmap more precise.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text: Mini-Challenge 2 presents a visual analytics problem centered on bias, incomplete evidence, and conflicting narratives. Two interest groups, FILAH and TROUT, accuse the COOTEFOO committee of opposing forms of economic bias, yet both rely on partial datasets. This creates an ideal setting for visual analytics because the challenge is not just to inspect data, but to understand how different data samples can produce different stories. Our project helps a journalist explore these competing claims transparently, identify whether bias exists, and distinguish genuine behavioral patterns from distortions caused by missing or selective evidence.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text: Mini-Challenge 2 presents a visual analytics problem centered on bias, incomplete evidence, and conflicting narratives. Two interest groups, FILAH and TROUT, accuse the COOTEFOO committee of opposing forms of economic bias, yet both rely on partial datasets. This creates an ideal setting for visual analytics because the challenge is not just to inspect data, but to understand how different data samples can produce different stories. Our project helps a journalist explore these competing claims transparently, identify whether bias exists, and distinguish genuine behavioral patterns from distortions caused by missing or selective evidence.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text:
Data
The project uses the MC2 knowledge graph datasets:

FILAH_links.csv
FILAH_nodes.csv
TROUT_links.csv
TROUT_nodes.csv
journalist_links.csv
journalist_nodes.csv
geo_edges.csv
geo_nodes.csv

These are built from two main sources: meeting notes and travel records. The graph contains entities such as people, organisations, places, meetings, discussions, plans, topics, and trips, with attributes including date, time, role, sentiment, industry, latitude, and longitude. These datasets are linked through shared person and place records, allowing comparison between partial and complete views of the same committee activities.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text:
Scope of Work
Our scope covers four main components.

1. Data Preprocessing and Preparation
The CSV datasets (FILAH, TROUT, and journalist datasets) will be preprocessed and designed into relational tables suitable for analysis in Tableau. This includes cleaning of attributes such as dates, locations, and sentiment values, and structuring the data to enable efficient querying and visualization. We will also prepare geospatial data for mapping COOTEFOO members’ travel patterns and meeting locations.


2. Dashboard Design and Development
Interactive Tableau dashboards will be designed to support multiple analytical perspectives. These include dashboards for committee overview, member-level behavior, travel and location patterns, topic and sentiment analysis, and comparisons between the partial datasets (FILAH and TROUT) and the full journalist dataset.


3. Visual Analytical Workflow Development
We will develop visual analytics workflows that allow users to interactively explore the data and trace accusations back to the supporting or missing evidence. These workflows will enable users to examine differences between datasets, investigate individual committee members, and identify patterns that may indicate bias or incomplete information.


4. Analysis and Interpretation of Findings
The final stage will synthesize analytical findings into a clear narrative explaining whether observed bias in COOTEFOO member behavior is genuine, exaggerated, or influenced by sampling limitations. The project will focus on explanation, comparison, and interpretive visual analysis rather than predictive modeling.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project

title: Results and Analysis
text:
Central finding: Neither FILAH’s nor TROUT’s accusations are fully supported by the complete evidence. Both groups curated their datasets to manufacture a narrative: selecting records that supported their case and quietly omitting those that contradicted it. When viewed through the full journalist dataset, the COOTEFOO board shows near-balanced engagement with both fishing and tourism industries.



What makes this finding analytically interesting is that both groups were working from the same pool of evidence. This was not two independent observers reaching different conclusions. It was two groups with opposing agendas selectively extracting from a shared record.

title: Results and Analysis
text:
Central finding: Neither FILAH’s nor TROUT’s accusations are fully supported by the complete evidence. Both groups curated their datasets to manufacture a narrative: selecting records that supported their case and quietly omitting those that contradicted it. When viewed through the full journalist dataset, the COOTEFOO board shows near-balanced engagement with both fishing and tourism industries.



What makes this finding analytically interesting is that both groups were working from the same pool of evidence. This was not two independent observers reaching different conclusions. It was two groups with opposing agendas selectively extracting from a shared record.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q1 — Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s own datasets support their accusations?
text:
Q1 — Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s own datasets support their accusations?






Answer: Yes, but the support is manufactured through selective inclusion.




What each group’s data shows
When analysed in isolation, both datasets appear to support their respective accusations:

FILAH’s data shows board members engaging with Tourism topics at a mean sentiment of 0.740 versus Fishing topics at only 0.209. This appears to validate FILAH’s claim that the board is biased toward tourism.
TROUT’s data shows the exact inverse: Fishing sentiment of 0.720 versus Tourism sentiment of only 0.141. This appears to validate TROUT’s claim of pro-fishing bias.

These are not small differences. They look like compelling evidence.
The dashboard below places all three datasets side by side: the trellis (top left) shows how dramatically sentiment shifts depending on whose records you use, and the Accusations vs Reality chart (bottom right) makes the overstatement explicit.



Figure Q1.1 — Sentiment Overview Dashboard. Top row: sentiment bar chart trellis comparing mean sentiment by industry across FILAH (Fishing=0.209, Tourism=0.740), Journalist (Fishing=0.490, Tourism=0.549), and TROUT (Fishing=0.720, Tourism=0.141).  Bottom left: Topic coverage heatmap showing frequency of mentions per topic across all three datasets. Bottom right: Accusations vs Reality chart directly comparing each group’s claimed sentiment against the full journalist baseline.





The heatmap evidence
The topic coverage heatmap reveals the mechanism behind the distortion. The two circled cells below are the smoking gun:

FILAH recorded zero links for concert: a Tourism topic, while accusing the board of pro-tourism bias.
TROUT recorded zero links for seafood_festival: a Fishing topic, while accusing the board of pro-fishing bias.

These are not gaps from incomplete data collection. Both groups removed the topic that most weakened their argument.



Figure Q1.2 — Topic Coverage Heatmap.





The accusations vs reality
Comparing each group’s claimed sentiment values against the full journalist baseline makes the overstatement measurable:




FILAH claimed
Full reality
Overstatement




Tourism sentiment
0.74
0.55
+0.19


Fishing sentiment (TROUT)
0.72
0.49
+0.23



Each group overstated the apparent bias by approximately 0.2 sentiment points through selective inclusion alone, not by fabricating numbers, but by choosing which records to show.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q2 — Is the COOTEFOO board actually biased?
text:
Q2 — Is the COOTEFOO board actually biased?






Answer: No. The full dataset shows near-equal engagement with both industries at the board level.




Board-wide sentiment
Using the complete journalist dataset across all 6 members:



Fishing
Mean sentiment = 0.490
43 positive / 10 negative interactions


Tourism
Mean sentiment = 0.549
58 positive / 6 negative interactions



A difference of 0.059 across a −1 to +1 scale does not constitute meaningful bias. Both industries receive positive engagement.
The chart below shows Board Sentiment Direction (top) and member participation breakdown (bottom) using the full journalist dataset. The green bars vastly outweigh the red across both industries.



Figure Q2.1 — Board Sentiment Direction and Member Participation. Top: Board Sentiment Direction showing the net count of positive (green) vs negative (red) board interactions by industry — both Fishing and Tourism are overwhelmingly positive, with no meaningful directional imbalance. Bottom: Member participation stacked bar showing how each member’s total participation links are distributed across Fishing (orange), Tourism (blue), and Other (grey) topics.


The trellis below reinforces this — placing FILAH, Journalist, and TROUT side by side shows that the Journalist dataset sits squarely between the two partisan extremes.



Figure Q2.2 — Sentiment Bar Chart Trellis. Mean sentiment by industry across FILAH, Journalist, and TROUT datasets placed side by side. FILAH’s data shows Tourism dominating at 0.740 vs Fishing at 0.209; TROUT’s shows the inverse at Fishing=0.720 vs Tourism=0.141. The full Journalist dataset sits between both at Fishing=0.490, Tourism=0.549.





Member-level variation exists — but it is not systemic
Individual members do show different industry orientations:









Member
Fishing sentiment
Tourism sentiment
Profile




Tante Titan
0.75
0.47
Broadly positive across both


Ed Helpsford
1.00
0.00
Fishing-positive, tourism-neutral


Carol Limpet
—
0.68
Tourism-leaning


Simone Kat
0.12
0.91
Strongly tourism-leaning


Teddy Goldstein
0.85
−0.50
Only member with negative industry sentiment


Seal
0.20
0.20
Low but balanced



The individual variation is real — but it is variation between members, not a systematic board-level lean.



Figure Q2.3 — Member vs Board Average. Each bar represents a member’s mean sentiment for a given industry (Fishing = orange, Other = grey, Tourism = blue), plotted against the board-wide average line of 0.430. Teddy Goldstein’s negative tourism bar (−0.50) is the sole instance of negative industry sentiment on the entire board.





Figure Q2.4 — Member × Topic Sentiment Heatmap. Each cell represents a member’s participation in a given topic, coloured by industry (Fishing = orange, Tourism = blue, Other = grey). The spread of squares across both Fishing and Tourism columns confirms that no member is exclusively engaged with one industry.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q3 — Are the accusations weakened by the full dataset?
text:
Q3 — Are the accusations weakened by the full dataset?






Answer: Yes, substantially — for both groups.




What changed and why
The sentiment scores for individual members do not change between datasets. What changes is who is present and what topics are covered — and this shifts the overall picture dramatically.



FILAH excluded:
Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.85)
Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0)
Tante Titan (Fishing=0.75)
→ These three are precisely the strongest pro-fishing members. Their absence drove FILAH’s fishing average down to 0.209.


TROUT excluded:
Simone Kat (Tourism=0.91)
Carol Limpet (Tourism=0.68)
Tante Titan (Tourism=0.47)
→ These three are precisely the strongest pro-tourism members. Their absence drove TROUT’s tourism average down to 0.141.





The suppression numbers




Missing from FILAH
Missing from TROUT




Fishing participation links
28
32


Tourism participation links
30
47


Other participation links
38
41









TROUT suppressed 47 tourism participation links — more than any other category from either organisation. This is the clearest quantification of editorial bias in the dataset.






Figure Q3.1 — Member × Topic Sentiment Heatmap and Member vs Board Average. Left: heatmap showing each member’s topic-level engagement grouped by industry — gaps indicate topics with no participation in that dataset view. Right: Member vs Board Average comparing each member’s mean sentiment against the board-wide average of 0.430. Teddy Goldstein’s −0.50 tourism bar and Simone Kat’s near-zero fishing sentiment are the two most notable departures.





The travel evidence
FILAH recorded 15 of 22 travel links. TROUT recorded only 4 of 22. The near-total absence of travel records from TROUT’s dataset is striking — the geographic dimension of board member activity was almost entirely suppressed. Of FILAH’s 15 recorded travel links, 7 were to industrial zones associated with fishing activity, directly contradicting their claim of pro-tourism bias.



Figure Q3.2 / Q4.1 — Travelled Destinations and Evidence Missing by Organisation. Left: Travelled Destinations showing travel records contributed per dataset by industry and zone type — TROUT’s near-absence is immediately visible. Right: Evidence Missing by Organisation quantifying participation links absent from each partial dataset relative to the full journalist record.





Tante Titan — the suppressed member






Tante Titan has 54 participation records in the journalist dataset — more than any other member. Her engagement is broadly positive (Fishing=0.75, Tourism=0.47, Other=1.0). Neither FILAH nor TROUT included a single one of her records.



She does not fit either narrative. She is not dramatically pro-tourism (which would support FILAH’s case) nor dramatically pro-fishing (which would support TROUT’s case). Her balanced profile made her inconvenient for both groups, so both groups removed her entirely.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q4 — How does an individual member’s story change across datasets?
text:
Q4 — How does an individual member’s story change across datasets?






Answer: Dramatically — the same person can look like a partisan, a moderate, or simply not exist, depending on whose dataset you use.




The most demonstrative case: Teddy Goldstein
Teddy appears only in TROUT’s dataset. Switching the Dataset View reveals:

In TROUT’s view: Teddy is visible. Fishing=0.85, Tourism=−0.50. He looks like a fishing partisan and provides strong apparent support for TROUT’s accusation.
In FILAH’s view: Teddy does not exist. FILAH recorded none of his 22 participation records.
In Full Journalist: Same values as TROUT (0.85 / −0.50) — the full dataset does not add new Teddy records, it adds other members who counterbalance him.

TROUT showed Teddy because his profile fit their narrative. FILAH hid him because his pro-fishing sentiment would have undermined their claim.


The most dramatic absence: Tante Titan
Selecting Tante Titan and switching to FILAH or TROUT view produces empty charts. Switching to Full Journalist reveals 54 records with a balanced, positive profile. The contrast between empty panels and populated full-dataset panels is the clearest single illustration of deliberate suppression in the entire project.
The trellis below makes this visible — members absent from a dataset simply do not appear in that panel. The missing rows are the story.



Figure Q4.2 — Mean Sentiment by Member and Industry Across Datasets. Trellis showing each member’s mean sentiment across FILAH Member Inclusion (left), Journalist Member Inclusion (centre), and TROUT Member Inclusion (right). Fishing = orange, Tourism = blue, Other = grey. Tante Titan is entirely absent from both the FILAH and TROUT panels despite having 54 records in the full journalist dataset.

title: Results and Analysis
text:
Limitations of Evidence






CautionSentiment as a proxy





Sentiment scores are assigned in the original dataset and we treat them as given. The scoring methodology is not documented. Whether a 0.5 vs 0.75 distinction is analytically meaningful is uncertain. We use sentiment as a directional indicator, not a precise measurement.

Small sample sizes in some comparisons
The travel link analysis covers only 22 rows total, with TROUT’s contribution being just 4. Percentage-based comparisons at this scale are unreliable — we deliberately chose raw counts for the travel chart for this reason.


Topic classification is interpretive
The assignment of topics to Fishing, Tourism, or Other was made by us based on topic descriptions. affordable_housing in particular could reasonably be classified differently. This classification affects industry-level sentiment averages.


No causal inference
We can show that each group’s data produces a biased picture and that they excluded records that would have moderated their claims. We cannot prove intent — it is possible, if unlikely, that the omissions were accidental. The pattern is consistent enough to be analytically suspicious, but a journalist like Moray would want to treat this as a hypothesis to investigate further rather than a proven conclusion.


Temporal analysis not performed
The links files contain a time column, but dates are stored inconsistently. Timeline analysis was not attempted. It is possible that the pattern of board activity changed over time in ways that would complicate the industry-level averages we report. This is a gap worth noting.

title: Challenge
text:
Fishing is Living and Heritage (FILAH) is an activist group representing the interests of Oceanus’s traditional fishing economy. They believe that COOTEFOO: the government oversight board tasked with advising on Oceanus’s economic future, have pro-tourism sentiment, and that board members are systematically neglecting the fishing industry that has historically powered the region. FILAH submitted a dataset of 765 links as evidence, to show board members engaging positively with tourism-related discussions while appearing indifferent or hostile toward fishing.
Tourism Raises Oceanus Together (TROUT) is an activist group on the opposite side of the same dispute. They argue that COOTEFOO has been focused on fishing, and that board members are dragging their feet on the economic opportunities that tourism represents for Oceanus’s future. TROUT submitted a dataset of 378 links as their counter-evidence, to show board members favouring fishing-related activity and ignoring tourism.
VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 2 presents a bias investigation framed around a fictional coastal town, Oceanus. The town’s oversight board, COOTEFOO, has been accused of economic bias by two opposing activist groups: FILAH (pro-fishing) and TROUT (pro-tourism), who each submitted datasets as evidence. A journalist, Edwina Darling Moray, has obtained both datasets alongside additional records of her own.
The task uses visual analytics to determine whether the accusations are supported by evidence, whether that evidence is trustworthy, and what the complete picture looks like once all available records are considered.

title: Challenge
section: The four questions we needed to answer
text:
The four questions we needed to answer








Question




Q1
Based on the datasets that TROUT & FILAH have provided, use visual analytics to determine if each group’s accusations are supported by their own record set. Is there evidence of bias in the COOTEFOO member actions in either dataset?


Q2
Is the committee as a whole biased? Provide visual evidence for your conclusions.


Q3
Are the accusations of TROUT strengthened, weakened or unchanged when taken in context of the whole dataset?


Q4
Design a visualization that allows Ms. Moray to pick a person and highlight the differences in that person’s behavior as illustrated through the different datasets.
